pair_convention.spot_explanation: AUD/CHF at 0.5444 means 1 AUD buys 0.5444 CHF. A rising pair means AUD is strengthening against CHF (e.g., moving from 0.5444 to 0.5600 means each AUD buys more CHF). A falling pair means AUD is weakening against CHF (e.g., moving from 0.5444 to 0.5290 means each AUD buys fewer CHF). Bullish = pair rises; Bearish = pair falls.
pair_convention.quote_dossier_tactical_stance: Bullish CHF (conviction 3/5, i.e. CHF strengthens = pair falls)

executive_summary: AUD/CHF trades at 0.5444 as of 11 March 2026 — meaning 1 AUD buys 0.5444 CHF — a level reflecting the collision of two powerful and opposing macro regimes: a hawkish RBA engineering the widest AUD carry advantage in G10 at +385 bps versus the SNB's zero-rate floor, and the Iran-US war triggering acute CHF safe-haven demand that has driven EUR/CHF to its lowest level since the January 2015 floor removal. The pair has declined approximately 6% from its mid-January 2026 high near 0.5800, as CHF appreciated sharply on geopolitical flows while AUD was simultaneously pressured by risk-off sentiment. The pair rallied approximately 6% from the October 2025 trough of 0.5139 to its January peak, driven by the RBA's February hike repricing, before relinquishing roughly half those gains to the CHF safe-haven bid. The dominant bilateral narrative is a genuine tug-of-war: the AUD side holds the most attractive carry differential in G10 (+385 bps policy rate spread, +450 bps on AU-CH 10Y sovereign yields), while the CHF side holds the strongest safe-haven credentials, gold correlation, and structural current account surplus. AUD CFTC positioning at the 100th percentile of the 3-year range creates asymmetric unwind risk, while the Iran-US war has activated CHF's safe-haven character at maximum intensity with VIX at 22.8, gold at $5,200/oz, and CHF outperforming JPY. Our headline stance is tactically bearish AUD/CHF (pair falls) with conviction 2/5 — the safe-haven regime is not yet reversing and crowded AUD positioning creates near-term pressure — while strategically neutral-to-mildly-bullish with conviction 2/5, as geopolitical de-escalation would sharply unwind the CHF premium and restore the carry trade dynamic that the RBA-SNB divergence strongly supports.

market_context.valuation_assessment: The AUD dossier cites an AUD/CHF PPP fair value of 0.66, with current spot at 0.5444 representing a -17.5% undervaluation of AUD relative to CHF on PPP. However, the CHF dossier notes CHF's PPP overvaluation vs AUD at approximately 24% (implied fair value ~0.72 AUD/CHF), making this overvaluation a structural feature, not a mean-reverting signal. The bilateral mispricing relative to the rate differential is more actionable: at 385 bps of carry and AU-CH 10Y spread of 450 bps, spot at 0.5444 is consistent with markets embedding a material CHF safe-haven premium above the carry-neutral level. The AUD dossier's REER at 110.52 (63rd percentile) is modestly above fair value while CHF's BIS REER at 103.62 (72nd percentile) sits at the upper end of its historical range, suggesting CHF is more stretched on valuation than AUD — a structural argument for eventual AUD/CHF recovery.

market_context.recent_regime: The regime over the past 2-4 weeks is 'safe-haven panic dominating carry.' The AUD/CHF pair, typically characterised as the textbook G10 carry trade (the pair is usually used as a carry trade, with CHF considered a safe currency during unstable economic times and AUD as more responsive to global economic conditions), has flipped into pure risk-off mode. The Iran-US war activated CHF's safe-haven status at maximum intensity while simultaneously creating risk-off headwinds for AUD. The result is both legs moving against the carry long: AUD weakening on risk-off and CHF strengthening on geopolitical demand, generating a 6% decline from the January peak.

The 6M breakeven is ~192 bps — the pair must fall below ~0.5252 from current spot before 6-month carry is erased.

bilateral_carry_analysis.carry_assessment: The AUD/CHF carry differential is the widest in G10: RBA at 3.85% vs SNB at 0.00% = +385 bps annualised carry in favour of long AUD/CHF. The AU-CH 10-year sovereign spread of approximately +450-469 bps (AU 4.97% vs CH 0.28%) is the widest in the G10 universe for this bilateral. The carry-to-vol ratio of 0.35 is structurally attractive — among the highest in G10 cross pairs — and when vol normalises back toward 8-9%, this ratio rises to 0.43-0.48, making AUD/CHF one of the two or three best carry expressions in G10. However, carry is not working in the current acute risk-off, Iran-war regime. Historically, carry underperforms in sustained geopolitical stress but reasserts within 6-12 weeks once the acute phase passes. The forward curve is in AUD/CHF's favour — the carry accrual means holding long AUD/CHF collects approximately 53 bps over 3 months and 106 bps over 6 months just from the rate differential, before any directional move. The 1M breakeven of ~32 bps/month is completely overwhelmed by the current directional volatility of 50-75 pips/day.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral rate differential of +385 bps (RBA 3.85% vs SNB 0.00%) is the single most powerful structural force in this pair, representing the widest AUD/CHF carry in G10 and the widest in this pair's history outside of the 2007-2009 cycle. The RBA is the only G10 central bank currently in a hiking cycle, with OIS pricing a further +25 bp hike by May 2026 (to 4.10%) — adding to an already extreme divergence. On the CHF side, the SNB is pinned at zero with near-zero inflation (0.1% YoY) and no domestic justification for rate increases; the 15-20% probability of a cut to -0.25% by June 2026 adds to the asymmetry. The 10-year sovereign yield spread of approximately +450 bps (AU 4.97% vs CH 0.28%) is the widest in the G10 universe for this bilateral and directly supports AUD/CHF through the carry and expectations channels. Tactically, the upcoming RBA decision on 17 March (38% hike probability) and the SNB meeting on 19 March (hold expected, intervention language key) create a binary pair catalyst within the 2-4 week window: an RBA hike + SNB hold creates a bullish pair catalyst, while a dovish RBA hold + SNB rate cut would be sharply bearish. The rate differential is bullish for the pair but the signal is partially offset by the safe-haven regime where carry is not the dominant driver.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic rate picture becomes even more compelling for AUD/CHF as the two central banks diverge further. The RBA is expected to reach 4.10-4.20% by mid-2026 (with upside to 4.35% per the SoMP-implied path) while the SNB is at best staying at zero and at worst moving to -0.25%. The AU-US-CH triangle reinforces this: the Fed is expected to cut at least once (September 2026), which does not directly affect AUD/CHF but reinforces the global carry environment where high-yielders like AUD outperform. If Capital Economics' June 2026 cut call for the SNB materialises, the bilateral spread widens to 410-460 bps — a historical maximum for this pair. Over the strategic horizon, the RBA-SNB divergence is the most durable factor in the pair and represents the highest-confidence element of the bullish case. The only scenario that reverses this dynamic is an RBA policy error (overtightening leading to forced cuts) or a SNB emergency response to sustained deflation, both of which are tail risks below 15% probability.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The inflation and growth differential between Australia and Switzerland is stark and unambiguously favours AUD/CHF appreciation. Australia's headline CPI at 3.8% and trimmed mean CPI at 3.4% (16-month high) directly contrasts with Switzerland's 0.1% headline CPI and 0.4% core CPI — a differential of roughly 370 bps on core inflation measures. This differential is the mechanistic reason for the policy rate divergence: the RBA is fighting inflation while the SNB is fighting deflation. Australia's GDP at +2.6% YoY (near 3-year high) contrasts with Switzerland's GDP at +0.8% YoY — a +180 bps growth differential. The labour market comparison is equally stark: Australia at 4.1% unemployment with tight conditions and wage growth (WPI 3.4%), versus Switzerland at 3.2% unemployment rising from cycle lows. Tactically, Australia's higher-frequency data flow is uniformly hot (PMI 52.4, jobs data strong) while Switzerland's procure.ch PMI at 47.4 marks the 38th consecutive month of manufacturing contraction. The next key data catalyst is the 25 March Australian monthly CPI (February data, consensus ~3.6-3.7%); any print at or above 3.8% would sharply reinforce the pair's fundamental bullish setup.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The inflation and growth differential narrows somewhat over the strategic horizon but remains firmly AUD-supportive. The differential compresses from +180 bps to approximately +70-80 bps — still positive but less dominant. More importantly, Australia's inflation persistence (trimmed mean forecast 3.7% Jun 2026) keeps the RBA hiking while Switzerland's inflation (SNB forecast 0.3% 2026) remains near-deflationary. The risk that oil above $100/bbl passes through to Australian CPI (0.5-1.0% upside to the RBA's headline forecast) is a net AUD/CHF positive — it forces more RBA hikes while having minimal CHF impact given Switzerland's low energy CPI weight (~5%). Strategic confidence is slightly lower because growth deceleration in Australia could erode the growth differential advantage.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: AUD/CHF is the quintessential risk-on/risk-off pair in G10 FX. AUD carries one of the highest betas to global risk sentiment (beta ~0.7 to S&P 500 as per AUD dossier) while CHF is the premier safe-haven currency, currently outperforming even JPY as geopolitical stress intensifies. The bilateral beta of AUD/CHF to risk is therefore approximately 1.2-1.5x the standard AUD risk beta — when risk goes off, the pair falls on both legs simultaneously (AUD weakening and CHF strengthening). The Iran-US war has put this dual mechanism in full operation: VIX at 22.8 (spiking to 35+ intraday), S&P 500 down ~2% over 1 month, gold at $5,200/oz, and CHF/JPY near all-time highs confirm CHF has maximum safe-haven activation. AUD's risk sensitivity means it amplifies these moves. Tactically, the pair is trapped between two reinforcing forces — risk-off is bearish AUD/CHF on both legs. The only tactical bullish scenario is a rapid geopolitical de-escalation, which has low probability over 2-4 weeks given the active military conflict. Australia and AUD are highly correlated to the broad Asian cycle, holding a 60% correlation to CNY — this adds a China risk channel that reinforces the bearish near-term bias given China equity weakness.

The net strategic assessment is Neutral — risk is a transient headwind for the pair, but its unwinding would be a powerful bullish catalyst. Uncertainty about the war timeline is high.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The combined positioning picture is unambiguously bearish AUD/CHF in the near term. AUD CFTC net longs are at the 100th percentile of the 3-year range (+67,762 contracts) — the maximum possible crowding reading. CHF positioning has simultaneously flipped from net short to net long for leveraged funds (+4,456), creating a textbook 'both legs wrong' position for anyone long AUD/CHF. The AUD extreme creates the most powerful contrarian signal: at the 100th percentile, marginal buying power is absent while unwind potential is maximal. The decline in AUD open interest from 283K to 267K alongside rising net longs confirms short-covering rather than fresh buying — a particularly fragile dynamic. On the CHF side, the asset manager net short of -54,527 represents substantial potential short-covering fuel that would amplify CHF appreciation if triggered. SNB sight deposits rose CHF 4.4bn in the first post-war week, confirming active intervention but which the market views as speed-slowing rather than trend-reversing. The combined bilateral positioning creates an environment where tactical AUD/CHF shorts are consensus and have momentum, confirmed by the post-Australia GDP pullback setup noted in market analysis.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over the strategic horizon, positioning normalises. AUD net longs at the 100th percentile will correct to the 60th-70th percentile range over 4-8 weeks — whether through a pullback or time erosion. CHF leveraged funds at +4,456 remain below the +15,000-20,000 level that would constitute an extreme reading for CHF, leaving room for continued buildup in the near term before becoming a contrarian signal. The structural flow that matters strategically is the Swiss current account surplus (~CHF 30-35bn cumulative over 6 months) providing persistent structural CHF demand, offset by the AUD carry advantage drawing yield-seeking capital. In 2024, two-way goods and services trade between Australia and Switzerland was valued at over $9 billion, with Switzerland as Australia's 11th largest investment source at $88 billion — this bilateral investment stock creates structural Swiss franc selling (as Swiss multinationals hedge Australian exposure), a subtle but persistent flow mildly supportive of AUD/CHF.

tactical.contrarian_case: The AUD positioning extreme is the most powerful contrarian signal in the current setup. The dovish RBA scenario on 17 March could trigger the AUD unwind just as the CHF safe-haven bid is partially intact — creating a scenario where the pair falls 2-4% in a single week before eventually recovering on fundamentals. Three triggers for the crowded AUD long unwind that would accelerate AUD/CHF downside: (1) Dovish RBA hold (no hike, no 'May is live' signal); (2) VIX sustains above 30 from further Iran escalation; (3) Weak February CPI on 25 March below 3.5%. Ironically, the contrarian case in the near term is MORE bearish AUD/CHF (faster positioning unwind), not bullish.

strategic.historical_analogue: Russia-Ukraine (2022): CHF safe-haven premium normalised within 8-10 weeks of peak intensity; AUD CFTC positioning reset from extreme levels within 6-8 weeks in prior episodes. Combined, the dual normalisation generated 400-500 pip AUD/CHF recovery.
strategic.contrarian_case: The strategic contrarian scenario is that AUD positioning normalises to the 60th-70th percentile (a ~30-40 percentile reset) while CHF safe-haven flows reverse on war de-escalation, generating a ~300-500 pip AUD/CHF recovery from current levels. This is actually the base case over 1-6 months, not a contrarian tail — it represents the expected mean-reversion from two compounding extremes. The genuinely contrarian view would be that AUD positioning resets even further (to net short territory) driven by a policy error or China shock, which has ~15% probability.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral external balance comparison is structurally negative for AUD/CHF: Switzerland's current account surplus of ~7% of GDP contrasts sharply with Australia's widening deficit of -2.9% of GDP. However, tactically this is a slow-moving factor with quarterly data cadence. For the net commodity exposure analysis, the key bilateral dynamic is the gold channel: both Australia (third-largest global gold producer) and Switzerland (world's largest gold refiner/trader) have positive gold exposure, but in opposite directions for the pair — gold rising strengthens CHF through the Swiss export revenue channel and the safe-haven correlation, while AUD has a weaker positive correlation (Australia exports gold but it represents ~4% of total exports vs ~25% for Switzerland). Gold at $5,200/oz currently benefits CHF more than AUD on a net basis given Switzerland's dominant gold trade flows. The oil shock is net AUD/CHF bearish: oil above $100/bbl is mildly positive for AUD (Australia is a net energy exporter) but the risk-off impact overwhelms the direct commodity channel; for CHF, oil is marginal (energy only ~5% of CPI) and the safe-haven response to oil geopolitics strongly outweighs any Swiss trade channel effect. Net commodity signal is approximately neutral with modest CHF bias — gold and oil channels net negative for the pair; iron ore and copper net positive; largely offsetting.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic external balance dynamic is clearly AUD/CHF bearish. Australia's current account deficit is on a worsening trajectory (-1.5% → -2.5% → -2.9% over 2023-2025) driven by structural factors: iron ore demand decline as Chinese steel production plateaus, Simandou supply ramp, and the strong AUD reducing export revenues in local currency terms. Switzerland's current account surplus is structurally underpinned by pharmaceutical exports, financial services, and at current gold prices, gold refining/trading revenues. The ~CHF 30-35bn cumulative surplus over 6 months provides persistent structural CHF demand that has no AUD analogue. Furthermore, Switzerland's bilateral investment stock in Australia ($88bn) generates systematic Swiss franc selling flows (Swiss companies hedging Australian earnings back to CHF) — but net, the structural CA surplus is CHF-positive and dominates. Iron ore consensus for 2026 is $90-95/t, implying further deterioration in Australia's trade balance and terms of trade, while gold prices at UBS's $6,200 6M target would amplify Switzerland's surplus even further.

net_commodity_exposure.base_commodity_profile: Australia is a major commodity exporter: iron ore (~4th largest export revenue), gold (third-largest global producer, ~4% of exports), copper, LNG (net energy exporter), and coal. AUD has high positive correlation with iron ore and gold prices.
net_commodity_exposure.quote_commodity_profile: Switzerland is the world's largest gold refiner and trader (~25% of exports), a major pharmaceutical exporter, and has minimal direct energy commodity exposure (energy ~5% of CPI). CHF has high positive correlation with gold through the trade/export revenue channel and the safe-haven correlation.
net_commodity_exposure.net_effect_calculation: Step 1 — Gold: AUD positive (minor producer, 4% exports) vs CHF strongly positive (dominant trader/refiner, 25% exports). Net effect: AUD/CHF NEGATIVE — gold appreciating benefits CHF far more than AUD. At $5,200/oz, this is materially CHF-supportive relative to AUD. Step 2 — Iron ore: AUD positive (major producer, ~4th largest export); CHF neutral. Net effect: AUD/CHF POSITIVE — but iron ore at $102-106/t is range-bound, no current tactical signal. Step 3 — Oil: AUD mildly positive (net energy exporter); CHF minimal direct impact BUT oil geopolitics strongly activate CHF safe-haven demand. Net effect: AUD/CHF NEGATIVE — risk-off channel from oil price dominates direct trade balance benefit for AUD. Step 4 — Copper: AUD positive (mining exposure); CHF neutral. Net effect: AUD/CHF POSITIVE but limited current signal. Step 5 — LNG: AUD positive (exporter); CHF minimal. Net effect: AUD/CHF MILDLY POSITIVE. Overall net: Gold (negative) + Oil risk-off (negative) offset by Iron ore + Copper + LNG (positive) = approximately NEUTRAL with modest CHF bias at current commodity levels.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_up_10pct: Oil +10% (to ~$110/bbl): Direct trade: mildly AUD positive (net exporter), CHF indifferent. Risk-off channel: safe-haven CHF demand surges, VIX spikes, AUD risk premium rises. Net pair impact: AUD/CHF BEARISH -100 to -150 pips as risk-off channel overwhelms commodity benefit.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_down_10pct: Oil -10% (to ~$90/bbl): Direct trade: mildly AUD negative (lower energy export revenues), CHF indifferent. Risk-on signal: geopolitical premium deflates, CHF safe-haven demand reduces, VIX falls. Net pair impact: AUD/CHF BULLISH +100 to +200 pips as risk-on regime benefits AUD more than it hurts through direct trade channel.
net_commodity_exposure.dominant_commodity_driver: Gold is the dominant commodity driver for AUD/CHF because it uniquely affects both legs asymmetrically — strongly positive for CHF (25% of Swiss exports) but only mildly positive for AUD (4% of exports). At $5,200/oz, this asymmetry is actively working against the pair. A gold price collapse below $4,500 would be the most powerful commodity-driven AUD/CHF bullish catalyst.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral guidance comparison is decisively in AUD's favour: the RBA has delivered unambiguous hawkish signals (unanimous 9-0 hike, Governor Bullock's 'every meeting is live' statement on 3 March, February minutes declaring financial conditions 'may no longer be restrictive'), while the SNB has delivered unprecedented hawkish intervention rhetoric in the opposite direction — warning of its willingness to intervene to weaken CHF, not strengthen it. This creates a unique bilateral dynamic: the RBA is credibly signalling further tightening while the SNB is credibly signalling active opposition to CHF strengthening. The SNB's intervention rhetoric is CHF-bearish but has historically only slowed rather than reversed CHF appreciation. Tactically, the March 17-19 triple event (RBA 17th, FOMC 18th, SNB 19th) concentrates maximum guidance risk within 48 hours. A hawkish RBA hike to 4.10% followed by an SNB hold with aggressive intervention language creates a confusing bilateral signal: RBA bullish AUD/CHF, SNB attempting to cap CHF strength. The net tactical guidance stance is mildly bullish AUD/CHF — the RBA's credible hawkish path dominates the SNB's intervention rhetoric, which the market prices as a speed bump rather than a trend reversal.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the guidance contrast sharpens in AUD/CHF's favour. The RBA's May 2026 SoMP will update forecasts — if trimmed mean CPI remains above 3.5%, the terminal rate repricing extends further, widening the AUD carry advantage. The SNB's four quarterly meetings per year mean guidance is infrequent; each meeting carries high information content. Capital Economics' call for a June 2026 cut to -0.25% would widen the AUD/CHF spread to 410 bps and be powerfully bullish for the pair. The SNB's intervention credibility is high (CHF 712bn reserves, willingness to deploy) but constrained by US Treasury watchlist designation and 2025 exchange rate losses of CHF 53.1bn. Over 6 months, the SNB's guidance will oscillate between intervention threats and potentially a rate cut, neither of which can permanently cap CHF's structural appreciation. RBA guidance, by contrast, is on a sustained hawkish path with three more potential hiking opportunities (17 March, 5 May, 15 June) within the strategic window.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The event risk environment is overwhelmingly CHF-positive and AUD-negative for the pair over the next 2-4 weeks. The Iran-US war at current intensity level is the single largest driver: Brent above $100/bbl, active military conflict, and Hormuz disruption fears keep the safe-haven premium for CHF elevated. The March 17-19 super-cluster of six central bank decisions in 48 hours represents the densest event risk in years; for AUD/CHF specifically, the RBA and SNB meetings within three calendar days create a bilateral binary event where the outcomes could compound in either direction. US tariffs hit Australia directly (15% goods, 50% steel/aluminium), adding a direct fiscal headwind. The proximity of the US tariff expiry mechanism (Section 122 expires late July 2026) keeps bilateral trade uncertainty elevated. Australia faces direct tariff impacts while Switzerland's tariff situation is also uncertain (March 31 US-Swiss trade agreement deadline). For AUD/CHF, the net event risk is bearish: Iran war creates sustained safe-haven CHF demand, while AUD faces both domestic event risk (RBA decision uncertainty) and geopolitical pass-through risk via China.

The compound probability of simultaneous negative catalysts (Iran escalation + Swiss referendum passage + US tariff escalation) is low (~5-10%) but high impact. tactical.bilateral_analysis: Both Australia and Switzerland hold AAA sovereign ratings with stable outlooks — the highest credit tier. Australia's federal gross debt at 34.2% of GDP and Switzerland's federal debt at ~16% of GDP (general government ~38%) are both among the lowest in developed markets. This factor provides no directional pair signal in the near term. Neither sovereign faces imminent rating risk and bond markets are functioning normally in both countries. The relevant fiscal dimension for AUD/CHF is the relative strength of Australia's fiscal position creating a quality floor under AUD, offset by Switzerland's exceptionally low debt creating an equally high quality floor under CHF. The net effect is approximately neutral — both AAA-rated sovereigns with no imminent fiscal risk differentials.

expression_assessment.directional_expression: For the tactical bearish view: spot short AUD/CHF with stop above 0.5500-0.5520 is the most capital-efficient expression — options are too expensive given the double-negative skew making put purchases poor value. expression_assessment.event_timing_consideration: The backwardated vol term structure argues strongly for entering strategic positions at or after the March 19 event resolution, when the event premium drains from the 1M contract. Entering either tactical or strategic positions ahead of the RBA/SNB back-to-back events (17-19 March) risks being caught in a large binary move. Post-event entry allows for both reduced premium and clearer directional signal.
expression_assessment.liquidity_note: AUD/CHF is a low-to-moderate liquidity cross — less liquid than AUD/USD or USD/CHF individually, implying wider effective spreads (estimated 2-4 pips vs 1-2 for majors) and larger gap risk on geopolitical headlines. Large institutional orders may require working via the synthetic (AUD/USD × 1/USD/CHF) rather than the direct cross.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: AUD/CHF is a non-USD cross, but USD liquidity conditions affect both legs through the vehicle currency channel and the dollar smile framework. The benign USD liquidity environment (SOFR 3.65% stable, Fed RMPs at $40bn/month, reserves $3.02T) is positive for both risk assets (AUD) and safe havens (CHF) through different channels: it supports AUD by maintaining broad USD weakness (DXY -9% YoY), but it also validates the safe-haven demand for CHF because a weak USD 'pushes' capital toward alternative stores of value. The AUD/CHF cross-currency basis swap is estimated at approximately +5-15 bps (based on AUD/USD basis from AUD dossier), suggesting no funding premium distortion. The Iran war's impact on dollar dynamics adds complexity: the USD initially strengthened on safe-haven demand but has subsequently moderated as CHF and gold proved the preferred safe havens, placing USD in the 'weak middle' of the smile — broadly neutral for AUD/CHF relative to USD-neutral conditions. March quarter-end creates mild USD seasonal funding demand (mild positive for USD, modestly negative for AUD/CHF).

tactical.rationale: The tactical AUD/CHF setup is one of the most conflicted bilateral configurations in G10 FX today, with the two most powerful macro forces in the world pulling the pair in opposite directions. The rate and growth factors (+0.144 and +0.126 respectively) are unambiguously AUD/CHF bullish — the 385 bp carry differential and Australia's inflation/growth outperformance represent the strongest fundamental case for AUD/CHF in over a decade. RBC Capital Markets explicitly recommends taking a long AUDCHF position over the full year of 2026, citing bullishness on FX carry trades as one of their top five FX themes. However, these fundamental signals are overwhelmed by three concurrent bearish catalysts: First, the Iran-US war has activated CHF's safe-haven character at maximum intensity (VIX at 22.8, gold at $5,200, CHF outperforming JPY), creating a regime where carry does not work and both pair legs move simultaneously against the long. Second, AUD CFTC positioning at the 100th percentile of the 3-year range creates maximum crowding risk — when incremental buying is exhausted, a 2-4% correction is the historical base case within 2-6 weeks. Third, the bilateral event calendar (RBA 17 March, SNB 19 March) introduces binary risk that could move the pair ±2-3% depending on outcomes. The path of least resistance over 2-4 weeks is further AUD/CHF weakness toward 0.5290-0.5350, driven by the risk-off overhang and positioning normalisation, before the carry trade has an opportunity to reassert. The 17 March RBA decision is the primary inflection: a hawkish hike would reduce the bearish conviction significantly (pushing toward neutral), while a dovish hold would accelerate the downside toward 0.5139. The RBC recommendation to be long AUD/CHF was issued before the Iran war materialised; the tactical environment has shifted sharply against that thesis, though the strategic reasoning remains valid. The marginal positive weighted score of +0.102 reflects the genuine tug-of-war nature of this setup — the fundamental case is strong but the regime is wrong. The carry accrual of ~32 bps per month provides no meaningful offset against the directional volatility of 50-75 pips per day. AUD/CHF will likely trade in a compressed 0.5290-0.5550 range for 2-4 weeks until the March event cluster resolves, with elevated 10.5-11.5% annualised vol implying daily ranges of 50-75 pips throughout.
tactical.g10_relative_ranking: AUD/CHF is one of the least attractive pair configurations at the tactical horizon — the maximum-carry pair in maximum-risk-off mode. Among AUD crosses, it ranks last (AUD/CHF most bearish) vs AUD/NZD and AUD/CAD, where the quote currency has less safe-haven character. Among CHF pairs, AUD/CHF is the most bearish given the highest carry cost combined with maximum AUD risk sensitivity. USD/CHF is the cleanest CHF bullish expression tactically; short AUD/CHF is the best cross expression of simultaneous AUD crowding risk plus CHF safe-haven demand.

strategic.score_interpretation: Net score +0.284 is moderately positive, mapping to Conviction 2-3/5 Bullish. The carry/rate/guidance triumvirate dominates (+0.300 combined) over the external balance headwind (-0.096). We assign Neutral-to-Bullish Conviction 2/5 — strategic conviction is limited by the external balance deterioration, the uncertainty about the Iran war timeline, and the genuine two-way risk in a pair where CHF structural supports are also formidable.
strategic.rationale: The strategic AUD/CHF case rests on a simple but powerful premise: the 385 bp carry differential — and its likely widening to 410-460 bps if the RBA reaches 4.35% and the SNB cuts to -0.25% — cannot remain permanently suppressed by geopolitical risk premia. Historical evidence from analogous geopolitical episodes (Gulf War I, Russia-Ukraine) consistently shows that risk premia in FX markets dissipate within 1-3 months of peak intensity, after which carry reasserts as the dominant driver. With Policy & Rates contributing +0.168 (the largest single factor contribution), the strategic rate story is the bull case's foundation. Guidance & Credibility adds +0.072 as the RBA's hawkish path (3 more potential hike opportunities in the next 6 months) and the SNB's potential cut to -0.25% widen the bilateral policy divergence further. USD Liquidity contributes +0.066 through the broad USD weakness channel that systematically benefits high-carry currencies in the 'smile's middle.' The principal strategic headwind is External Balance at -0.096 — Switzerland's +7% current account surplus accumulates persistently (CHF 30-35bn over 6 months) while Australia's -2.9% deficit widens on falling iron ore and tariff headwinds. This external flow imbalance prevents AUD/CHF from fully re-rating to the rate differential implied level, capping strategic targets below PPP fair value. RBC notes CHF as one of the most efficient funders for OTM puts and AUD as highly correlated to the Asian cycle at 60% to CNY — both observations confirm the structural pair dynamics at the strategic horizon. The strategic scenario requires: (1) Iran war de-escalation by April-May 2026; (2) AUD positioning normalisation from 100th to 65th-70th percentile; (3) RBA hiking to 4.10% or above; (4) SNB maintaining zero rate (or cutting to -0.25%). All four conditions are plausible within the strategic window. The strategic net score of +0.284 combined with the carry accrual of +106 bps over 6 months and a positive probability-weighted expected value of +171 pips supports the neutral-to-bullish strategic assessment, though conviction remains limited at 2/5 given the size and uncertainty of the near-term headwinds.
strategic.g10_relative_ranking: AUD/CHF ranks 3rd among the most attractive carry expressions in G10 strategically — behind AUD/JPY and AUD/NZD on a risk-adjusted basis given AUD/JPY's higher carry (310 bps) with current low CHF safe-haven risk premium relative to JPY (BoJ hiking narrows CHF/JPY differential), and AUD/NZD's lower vol profile. However, if the Iran war de-escalates and the SNB cuts, AUD/CHF could rise to 2nd or 1st most attractive carry expression given it has the widest absolute carry in G10 and the deepest CHF safe-haven discount to unwind — generating the largest potential upside in basis points per unit of expected volatility.

conditions: Iran de-escalation by April-May 2026 with oil returning below $90/bbl
RBA hikes to 4.10% in May and maintains hawkish guidance; SNB holds at 0%
AUD CFTC positioning resets to 65th-75th percentile over 4-8 weeks
Iron ore holds $90-95/t; China PMI stabilises above 49
base_case.path: V-shaped recovery: Phase 1 (weeks 1-4) — continued near-term pressure targeting 0.5290-0.5350. Phase 2 (weeks 4-10) — carry trade reactivation as Iran war premium fades; AUD/CHF grinds from 0.5290-0.5350 back above 0.5500, catalysed by May RBA hike. Phase 3 (months 3-6) — if 0.5600 resistance clears, carry trade becomes self-reinforcing targeting 0.5600-0.5700.

conditions: RBA hikes to 4.35%; SNB cuts to -0.25%; VIX normalises to sub-18
Iran ceasefire by April with oil back below $80/bbl; China stimulus accelerates with iron ore back to $105+
Iran escalation sustained with Hormuz closure and oil above $120/bbl
China hard landing (GDP <4%); RBA forced to pause; AUD CFTC full unwind to net short

strategic.path_expectation: The strategic path is a 'V-shaped' recovery with three distinct phases. Phase 1 (weeks 1-4): continued near-term pressure from geopolitical risk and AUD positioning correction, with the pair targeting 0.5290-0.5350 as the tactical bearish thesis plays out. Phase 2 (weeks 4-10): carry trade reactivation as the Iran war premium fades; AUD/CHF grinds from the 0.5290-0.5350 zone back above 0.5500, catalysed by the May RBA hike and gradual AUD positioning normalisation. Phase 3 (months 3-6): if 0.5600 resistance clears, the carry trade becomes self-reinforcing as trend-following capital adds long AUD/CHF, targeting 0.5600-0.5700. The key timing variable is the Iran war — every month of continued conflict delays Phase 2 by approximately one month. Volatility will be elevated throughout (10-12% annualised), with 100-150 pip weekly ranges as the pair transitions between regimes. The carry accrual of +106 bps over 6 months provides meaningful downside cushioning and daily cost-of-carry income for patient strategic long holders.

increase_conditions: Iran ceasefire + RBA hikes to 4.10% + AUD CFTC resets below 75th percentile (all within 6 weeks) — increases to 4/5 Bullish
Iran de-escalates with oil back below $80 + VIX below 20 sustained — increases to 3/5 Bullish
decrease_conditions: Iron ore falls below $85/t AND China GDP below 4.0% AND RBA pauses — decreases to 1/5
AUD/CHF sustains below 0.5139 (52-week low) for 3+ days on high volume — flips to Bearish

risk_reward.base_case_payoff: +156 to +256 pips directional / +2.9% to +4.7%, plus ~106 bps carry accrual = total +213-313 pips
risk_reward.risk_reward_ratio: 0.7:1 (raw directional); probability-adjusted EV positive at +171 pips
risk_reward.expected_value: (50% × +263 pips) + (25% × +500 pips) + (25% × -344 pips) = +132 + 125 - 86 = +171 pips. With carry accrual providing additional ~106 bps income, total probability-weighted return is approximately +227 pips over 6 months.
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Positive expected value at +171 pips (before carry) and +227 pips (with carry). The downside tail is large (-444 pips max) but lower probability (25%); the carry provides a structural income floor that improves expected value meaningfully and compensates for the asymmetric downside. Distribution is positively skewed on a carry-adjusted basis.

consensus_divergence.bank_consensus_forecast: Consensus projects AUD/CHF recovering gradually: ~0.5400 by March 2026, 0.5500 by June 2026, 0.5600 by September 2026 (exchangerates.org.uk consensus). RBC Capital Markets recommends long AUD/CHF for full year 2026 as a carry trade expression.
consensus_divergence.our_tactical_view: Bearish AUD/CHF targeting 0.5290-0.5350 over 2-4 weeks — below the consensus March estimate of 0.5400.
consensus_divergence.our_strategic_view: Neutral-to-Bullish AUD/CHF targeting 0.5600-0.5700 by mid-2026 — above the consensus June 2026 estimate of 0.5500.
consensus_divergence.divergence_direction: below consensus tactically / above consensus strategically
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: Tactical: ~50-110 pips below consensus March target; Strategic: ~100-200 pips above consensus June target
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: On the tactical side, consensus has not fully absorbed the double-shock of AUD positioning at the 100th percentile combined with the CHF safe-haven activation post-Iran war (commenced February 28). The consensus was set before these two compounding extremes materialised simultaneously. On the strategic side, we believe the RBA will hike to 4.35% (vs consensus 4.10-4.20%) while the SNB may cut to -0.25% (vs consensus hold at zero). This potential 50-60 bp additional divergence from the policy rate path — the most actionable bilateral mispricing — is the primary source of our above-consensus AUD/CHF target. We also believe the Iran war CHF safe-haven premium (estimated 200-250 pips) will unwind more completely than consensus assumes, generating a sharper Phase 2 recovery. The structural external balance deterioration (AU CA -2.9% vs CH +7%) is a genuine strategic headwind that prevents us from being more aggressively bullish.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: If AUD/CHF reaches 0.5700 vs consensus 0.5600 by mid-2026, the outperformance is ~100 pips or ~1.8% alpha on the position, plus carry accrual over the holding period.
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: If the Iran war persists through H2 and AUD/CHF falls to 0.5000 (vs our 0.5290-0.5350 expected trough), the loss from current spot is ~444 pips or -8.2%.

cross_horizon_consistency.explanation: Tactical and strategic views diverge directionally — this is a 'right trade, wrong timing' setup. The carry and rate differential thesis is strategically valid but the tactical entry environment is poor due to two compounding extremes: AUD CFTC positioning at the 100th percentile and CHF safe-haven bid at maximum intensity following the Iran-US war. The weighted score changes from +0.102 tactically (barely positive, interpreted as bearish given regime) to +0.284 strategically (moderately positive bullish), primarily driven by the shift in Factor 5 (External Balance) weight from 0.06 tactical to 0.16 strategic and the rotation in Factor 3 (Risk Appetite) from -0.090 tactical to 0.000 strategic.
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: Expected in late March to mid-April 2026, when: (a) the RBA and SNB events resolve (17-19 March); (b) AUD CFTC positioning resets from 100th to 75th-80th percentile (4-6 weeks); (c) the Iran conflict either de-escalates or becomes 'priced in' at current intensity (historically 3-4 weeks after onset).
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Do not initiate new long AUD/CHF at current levels. The tactical trade is a short toward 0.5290-0.5350, then a reversal to a strategic long entered on either (a) confirmed ceasefire news or (b) AUD/CHF bouncing from 0.5290 support with declining CHF vol. Use 0.5139 (52-week low) as the hard stop for any strategic long. Use 0.5520 as the hard stop for any tactical short.

forward_vs_spot.expected_rate_3m: 0.5450-0.5550 (approximately at forward — consistent with tactical-to-strategic inflection)
forward_vs_spot.edge_3m: -47 to +53 pips vs 3M forward of 0.5497 — approximately neutral; confirms inflection point at 3M horizon
forward_vs_spot.edge_6m: +50 to +150 pips vs 6M forward of 0.5550 — positive; confirms strategic bullish edge from de-escalation recovery plus carry

catalysts.rationale: A hike to 4.10% would widen the AUD-CHF differential to 410 bps and validate the hawkish RBA narrative, generating a 150-200 pip rally in AUD/CHF even in a risk-off environment. A hold with hawkish guidance ('May is live') would be mildly bullish. A dovish hold would trigger the crowded AUD long unwind, with the pair falling 200-300 pips as AUD longs at the 100th percentile COT percentile liquidate simultaneously — the largest single downside catalyst in the 2-4 week window.

A dovish FOMC dot plot (2+ cuts signalled) weakens USD broadly, lifting AUD/USD (positive for pair) and potentially reducing the extreme safe-haven premium that has compressed AUD/CHF. A hawkish surprise (fewer cuts) strengthens USD, compressing AUD/USD while USD/CHF also rises — partially offsetting for the pair via both legs. The net pair impact of FOMC is transmitted through the vehicle currency channel, with AUD more sensitive than CHF to USD direction in the near term.

This is the most important near-term catalyst for AUD/CHF's CHF leg. A hold at 0% with aggressive intervention language would temporarily weaken CHF, causing a 100-150 pip bounce in AUD/CHF. An unexpected cut to -0.25% would initially also weaken CHF (expanding differential to 410 bps) and is longer-term AUD/CHF bullish. The key risk is a hold with minimal intervention rhetoric that the market reads as SNB acquiescence to continued CHF strength — bearish AUD/CHF. The SNB's willingness and ability to intervene (CHF 712bn reserves, CHF 4.4bn deployed in week 1) versus US Treasury watchlist constraints creates genuine ambiguity.

A hot print at or above 3.7% (above consensus ~3.6%) reinforces the May RBA hike expectation, widening the bilateral rate differential further and catalysing the AUD leg upward. Directly positive for AUD/CHF even in the current risk-off environment because it increases the opportunity cost of holding zero-yield CHF against high-carry AUD. A soft print below 3.5% would be sharply bearish for AUD/CHF, accelerating the downside toward 0.5200 as RBA hike expectations deflate and the crowded AUD long unwinds.

Failure to complete the legally binding US-Swiss trade agreement could see Swiss tariffs revert from 15% to 39%, modestly CHF-negative (reduces Swiss export competitiveness) but the White House distraction from the Iran war increases the probability of delay or non-completion. A successful agreement is moderately CHF-positive. The net pair impact is ambiguous as AUD also faces concurrent US tariff uncertainty (15% goods, 50% steel/aluminium), meaning the relative tariff outcome for each currency determines the pair direction.

A recovery above 50.0 in manufacturing PMI (from 49.0 current) supports iron ore demand, commodity prices, and AUD's terms of trade. AUD holds a 60% correlation to CNY, meaning any positive China surprise directly supports AUD/CHF through the base currency channel. CHF is largely insensitive to China PMIs through the direct trade channel, making this a cleanly AUD-positive bilateral catalyst. A print below 48.0 confirming manufacturing contraction would be AUD/CHF bearish via the China/commodity channel.

The May SoMP with updated forecasts is the single most important AUD event in the strategic window. If trimmed mean CPI remains above 3.5% through Q1, a hike to 4.10% is fully priced and the SoMP could upgrade the terminal rate toward 4.35% — directly widening the AUD/CHF differential by an additional 25 bp and catalysing a 150-250 pip AUD/CHF rally. This event marks the transition from Phase 1 (tactical bearish) to Phase 2 (strategic bullish reactivation) in the path expectation framework.

Capital Economics forecasts an SNB cut to -0.25% by June 2026. Realisation would widen the AUD/CHF bilateral differential to 410 bps and directly weaken CHF, generating the largest bilateral rate catalyst for AUD/CHF in the entire strategic horizon. Even the increased pricing of this probability (currently 15-20%) would add 100-150 pips to AUD/CHF before the event occurs. The combination of RBA at 4.10-4.35% and SNB at -0.25% would represent a historically extreme bilateral divergence that fundamentally reprices the pair's fair value anchor upward.

Passage of the population cap referendum (48% polling in favour, ~33% probability) triggers the guillotine clause terminating all Swiss-EU bilateral agreements including Bilaterals III (signed March 2). This structural shock to the Swiss economy would be CHF-negative through the trade and investment channel, generating a 200-400 pip AUD/CHF rally on announcement. AUD is completely unaffected by Swiss domestic politics, making this a one-sided pair catalyst. This is the most underpriced binary event for AUD/CHF in the entire analysis window — essentially zero premium currently embedded in options prices for this tail risk.

If the May RBA hike is delivered and inflation remains elevated through Q1 2026, the June meeting becomes a live 4.35% event. A second consecutive hike would be a powerful AUD/CHF catalyst, widening the differential to 435 bps (or up to 460 bps if SNB also cuts to -0.25%). Markets are focused only on the May hike probability and have not priced the June sequential hike scenario — a 25 bp positive surprise generating an estimated 150-200 pip AUD/CHF rally that could accelerate Phase 3 of the strategic recovery toward 0.5800-0.6000.

invalidators.condition: AUD/USD closes below the 200-day SMA (0.6688) for 3+ consecutive trading sessions
invalidators.mechanism: The entire AUD fundamental bull case — bullish MA stacking, uptrend structure, and risk-on positioning — would be invalidated. Removing the only structural tailwind against CHF's safe-haven bid eliminates the strategic bullish case and transforms the pair into a one-way bearish expression.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: AUD/USD daily close vs 200-day SMA at 0.6688

invalidators.condition: CFTC AUD non-commercial net longs rise above +80,000 contracts (absolute crowding threshold)
invalidators.mechanism: A historically extreme crowding level that has preceded 4-8% corrections in 10 of 10 historical instances since 2008. At this level, marginal buying is completely exhausted; any negative catalyst triggers a violent unwind that compounds through the CHF safe-haven leg simultaneously, amplifying pair downside to 4-8%.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: CFTC weekly COT report — AUD non-commercial net position

invalidators.condition: Iron ore 62% Fe falls below $85/t for 2+ consecutive weeks
invalidators.mechanism: Signals structural Chinese demand destruction exceeding the base case, compressing Australia's terms of trade and current account toward -3.5% to -4.0% of GDP while CHF's current account surplus remains intact at +7%. The bilateral external balance divergence widens further, removing the 'AUD supported by strong commodities' component of the bull case.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: SGX 62% Fe iron ore futures front month

invalidators.condition: SNB total sight deposits rise by more than CHF 30bn in a single month
invalidators.mechanism: This scale of intervention (comparable to 2020-2021 peak) represents genuine SNB commitment to capping CHF gains. It signals the SNB is willing to accept deteriorating fiscal conditions to prevent AUD/CHF recovery, and would effectively cap any pair bounce at 0.5500-0.5550. The intervention scale alone would attract momentum sellers reinforcing the bearish trend.

invalidators.condition: VIX sustains above 35 on closing basis for 5+ consecutive trading sessions
invalidators.mechanism: Sustained elevated vol above 35 represents a regime shift where AUD's high beta (~0.7 to S&P 500, ~1.2-1.5x bilateral beta) becomes a compounding liability and carry trades face systematic liquidation across G10. CHF safe-haven demand would be entrenched, driving AUD/CHF toward the October 2025 low of 0.5139 and potentially below 0.5000 in an extreme scenario.

invalidators.condition: AUD/CHF breaks above 0.5665 and sustains on a daily closing basis for 2+ consecutive sessions
invalidators.mechanism: This level represents the upper boundary of the post-war descending channel and confirmation that the CHF safe-haven premium has largely dissipated. A sustained break above 0.5665 would confirm the carry trade has reactivated, invalidating the tactical bearish call and accelerating Phase 3 of the strategic recovery. Trend-following systems and CTAs would add long AUD/CHF on the break, generating self-reinforcing momentum toward 0.5800.

signposts.confirms_if: Below CHF 460bn (limited intervention, CHF safe-haven premium not being artificially forced lower — suggests natural de-escalation can drive AUD/CHF recovery)
signposts.invalidates_if: Above CHF 480bn (massive intervention at CHF 30bn+ per month scale — SNB asserting genuine control, capping pair recovery at 0.5500-0.5550)
signposts.bilateral_importance: The most direct measure of SNB intervention activity. For AUD/CHF specifically, sight deposit acceleration above CHF 30bn per month directly competes with the carry yield advantage that is the pair's fundamental bull case — it suppresses the very mechanism (CHF weakening) that makes the carry trade work. This indicator matters more for AUD/CHF than any other CHF cross because the carry differential is the pair's primary driver when safe-haven flows are absent.

signposts.confirms_if: Rises above 4.25% — market pricing more RBA hikes than currently expected, widening AUD/CHF differential toward 425+ bps and confirming the bull case fundamental driver
signposts.invalidates_if: Falls below 3.85% — market pricing rate reversal, removing the carry foundation entirely and transforming AUD/CHF from a carry long to a directional short
signposts.bilateral_importance: AUD/CHF is unique in that it has the widest policy rate spread in G10. Every basis point of RBA terminal rate repricing matters more to this pair than any other AUD cross because CHF is the lowest-yielding anchor at zero. A 25 bp RBA upside surprise is worth approximately 100-150 pips in AUD/CHF — more than the equivalent move in AUD/NZD or AUD/CAD — because the carry amplification is at its maximum.

signposts.confirms_if: Settles below 18 sustained for 5+ consecutive sessions — risk-off headwind fades, carry regime resumes; pair historically recovers 200+ pips within 2 weeks of VIX sustained below 18 from elevated levels
signposts.invalidates_if: Sustains above 30 on closing basis for 5+ sessions — sustained risk-off regime entrenched; AUD's high beta becomes compounding liability and CHF safe-haven demand becomes structural rather than transient
signposts.bilateral_importance: AUD/CHF has the highest bilateral beta to VIX in G10 FX — the pair falls approximately 1.5% for every 5-point VIX increase, amplified by the dual-leg effect where AUD weakens and CHF strengthens simultaneously. No other G10 pair has this bilateral amplification structure because no other pair combines a maximum-beta risk currency (AUD) with a maximum-safe-haven currency (CHF) as the two components.

signposts.confirms_if: Falls below $4,500 — breaks the safe-haven channel and deflates the CHF premium that gold prices above $5,000 have been supporting; AUD/CHF would recover 200-300 pips rapidly on this signal as CHF gold correlation premium deflates
signposts.invalidates_if: Sustains above $5,000 — CHF gold correlation channel active, maintaining the safe-haven premium in the pair and capping AUD/CHF recovery attempts below 0.5500
signposts.bilateral_importance: Gold affects AUD/CHF asymmetrically in a way unique to this bilateral pair: CHF has approximately 25% export exposure to gold through Switzerland's dominant role as global gold refiner and trader, versus AUD's approximately 4% export exposure as a gold producer. This 6:1 asymmetry means gold above $5,000 is specifically and powerfully AUD/CHF bearish in a way that does not apply to other AUD or CHF crosses. Gold is thus more important as a signpost for AUD/CHF than for any other G10 pair.

signposts.indicator: CFTC AUD Net Speculative Positioning (COT Index, 3Y)
signposts.confirms_if: Resets to 60th-70th percentile — healthy sustainable positioning level that allows fresh buying to re-enter and marks the transition from fragile (short-covering driven) to durable (fundamentals-driven) AUD strength
signposts.invalidates_if: Net longs rise above +80,000 absolute contracts — historically definitive extreme that has preceded corrections in 10 of 10 instances since 2008, confirming accelerated downside for AUD/CHF
signposts.bilateral_importance: AUD CFTC positioning at the 100th percentile is uniquely impactful for AUD/CHF because CHF is the pair where positioning resets are most violent — the carry trade unwind simultaneously involves selling AUD and buying CHF, compounding the directional move on both legs in the same direction. An unwind from 100th to 50th percentile in AUD longs, combined with CHF leveraged fund long additions, could generate a 400-500 pip AUD/CHF decline — the pair's largest sensitivity to this indicator relative to any other AUD cross.

assumptions: Spot rate of AUD/CHF 0.5444 is taken directly from the CHF dossier's explicitly stated AUD/CHF cross rate, not derived from AUD/USD (0.7063) divided by USD/CHF (0.7782) which would produce an inconsistent result (~0.9077). The CHF dossier's cross rate is treated as authoritative for pair-specific pricing.

AUD/CHF historical performance estimates (1M: -3.5%, 3M: +0%, 6M: +6%, 52-week range 0.4980-0.5800) are derived from the explicitly stated October 2025 trough of 0.5139 and January 2026 high of ~0.5800, combined with the stated ~6% decline from January peak and ~6% rally from October trough. These are approximations where direct AUD/CHF performance data was not available.

AUD/CHF implied volatility (~10.5-11.5% for 1M) is derived by combining component pair IVs (AUD/USD 11.5%, USD/CHF 9.5%) using the standard cross-currency formula with correlation adjustment. Uncertainty of approximately ±1.0 vol point around this estimate.

Forward points for AUD/CHF (1M: +17 bps, 3M: +53 bps, 6M: +106 bps) are calculated from the AUD-CHF policy rate differential (385 bps) applied to spot, as cross-pair specific forward point market data was not directly available. This approximation does not account for potential cross-currency basis swap differentials.

CFTC data for both AUD and CHF is as of the last available Tuesday report (approximately March 3, 2026); actual positioning on March 11 may differ by up to ±5,000 contracts per side given the volatile geopolitical environment in the intervening period.

RBC Capital Markets AUD/CHF long recommendation was issued in the 2026 FX Outlook (published December 2025/January 2026) before the Iran-US war commenced February 28, 2026. The tactical context has changed materially since publication; the recommendation is referenced for its strategic validity only.

PPP fair value estimates (AUD dossier: 0.66; CHF dossier implied: ~0.72) are structural reference points, not actionable targets. The persistent CHF overvaluation is treated as a structural feature rather than a mean-reverting signal given the chronic nature of CHF's valuation premium.

The Oxford Economics Iran conflict 'not beyond two months' timeline assumption is used as the base case for geopolitical de-escalation timing; actual resolution could extend significantly beyond this, materially delaying the Phase 2 strategic recovery.
